---
title: "Connectors are agents, not adapters"
description: "Extraction is interpretive work, so a connector is an agent that wraps a source, declares a capability, and files provenance; a small ontology per source is what lets it reason instead of guess."
date: "2026-07-20"
categories: [research-agents, connectors]
draft: false
---

::: {.callout-note title="Status"}
**Design, with a working proof of concept.** The general model is a design; one connector (PAD) has been built and run.
:::

**Thesis.** A connector is not a static adapter. It is an agent.

A static adapter is a fixed function: same input, same call, same shape out. Real extraction is nothing like that. Reading a table out of a paper, reconciling a supplementary spreadsheet against the paper's notation, negotiating a database's quirks, normalizing to a schema, attaching provenance and a confidence, retrying when a source is malformed, that is open-ended, interpretive work. So a connector is an agent that wraps a source, declares a capability, does the messy extraction, and files the result with its provenance. The researcher's hub agent discovers and orchestrates a fleet of them.

Calling it an "agent" is the risky half, though, because an agent that guesses is worse than no agent. The reliability mechanism is a **small ontology per source**. An LLM reasoning over an explicit small ontology (what the entities are, how they relate, what the fields mean) is accurate; an LLM inferring that structure from raw, unlabelled responses is measurably less accurate and often needs a human in the loop. So the ontology is part of the connector contract: a connector is not finished when it can call the API, it is finished when the source is described well enough for an agent to reason over it without guessing.

One more load-bearing idea: **discovery abstracts over delivery**. The agent discovers a capability, say "graph extraction," and invokes it without caring whether that resolves to a marketplace plugin it installs or a hosted service it calls. Delivery mode is an operations decision, never a user-facing one.

PAD (Paper Analytical Devices) is the first real candidate because it already self-describes, with both an OWL/RDF ontology and an OpenAPI spec. We read one to learn what it is and the other to learn how to call it, and [built the connector](../fn-pad-connector-poc/).
